metadata.title: Guidance for Industry: Premarketing Risk Assessment

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must conduct a systematic premarket safety evaluation as part of the drug development program to characterize the safety profile of a product before it is approved for marketing.
rules.source_section: Section I - Introduction
rules.verbatim_quote: The premarket safety evaluation should characterize the types and frequencies of adverse events that occur with the product; identify the risk factors that predispose patients to adverse events; determine if the adverse event is causally related to the product; estimate the magnitude of the risk associated with treatment; and compare risks between treatment groups in controlled clinical trials.

rules.rule_text: The safety database included in an NDA should generally include data from all clinical studies conducted by the sponsor, including all patients exposed to the drug at any dose for any duration.
rules.source_section: Section II - Size of the Safety Database
rules.verbatim_quote: The safety database included in an NDA should generally include data from all clinical studies conducted by the sponsor with the drug, including all patients exposed to the drug at any dose for any duration.
rules.exceptions: Phase 1 pharmacokinetic studies may sometimes be excluded from certain pooled analyses but should still be reported.

rules.rule_text: For drugs intended to treat non-life-threatening conditions and intended for chronic use, the safety database should generally include at least 1500 patients treated with the drug for the proposed indication.
rules.source_section: Section II - Size of the Safety Database
rules.verbatim_quote: For drugs intended to treat non-life-threatening conditions and intended for chronic use, we generally recommend that the safety database include at least 1500 patients treated with the drug for the proposed indication, with 300 to 600 of those patients treated for 6 months and 100 patients treated for at least 1 year.
rules.exceptions: Smaller databases may be acceptable for drugs intended to treat serious or life-threatening conditions, or drugs intended for short-term use.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors should conduct adequate long-term safety studies to support the proposed duration of treatment, with at least 100 patients treated for at least one year for drugs intended for chronic use.
rules.source_section: Section II - Size of the Safety Database
rules.verbatim_quote: 300 to 600 of those patients treated for 6 months and 100 patients treated for at least 1 year.
rules.exceptions: Not applicable to drugs intended for acute or short-term use.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must address potential drug-related QTc prolongation as part of the NDA for all new small molecule drugs.
rules.source_section: Section V.C - Addressing Safety Aspects During Product Development
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA recommends addressing the potential for the following serious adverse effects as a part of the new drug application (NDA) for all new small molecule drugs: Drug-related QTc prolongation.
rules.exceptions: A drug that is intended to be topically applied may be shown to have no systemic bioavailability; therefore, systemic toxicities would be of no practical concern.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must address drug-related liver toxicity as part of the NDA for all new small molecule drugs.
rules.source_section: Section V.C - Addressing Safety Aspects During Product Development
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA recommends addressing the potential for the following serious adverse effects as a part of the new drug application (NDA) for all new small molecule drugs: Drug-related liver toxicity.
rules.exceptions: Topically applied drugs with no systemic bioavailability may not require assessment of systemic toxicities.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must address drug-related nephrotoxicity, bone marrow toxicity, drug-drug interactions, and polymorphic metabolism as part of the NDA for all new small molecule drugs.
rules.source_section: Section V.C - Addressing Safety Aspects During Product Development
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA recommends addressing the potential for the following serious adverse effects as a part of the new drug application (NDA) for all new small molecule drugs: Drug-related nephrotoxicity; Drug-related bone marrow toxicity; Drug-drug interactions; Polymorphic metabolism.
rules.exceptions: Topically applied drugs with no systemic bioavailability may not require assessment of systemic toxicities.

rules.rule_text: For biological products such as cytokines, antibodies, and other recombinant proteins, sponsors must assess immunogenicity including both the incidence and consequences of neutralizing antibody formation and the potential for adverse events related to binding antibody formation.
rules.source_section: Section V.C - Addressing Safety Aspects During Product Development
rules.verbatim_quote: Potentially important issues for biological products include assessments of immunogenicity, both the incidence and consequences of neutralizing antibody formation and the potential for adverse events related to binding antibody formation.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors should ensure that each investigator's verbatim adverse event terms are coded to standardized, preferred terms specified in a coding convention or dictionary such as MedDRA throughout the clinical program.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A.1 - Accuracy of Coding
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA recommends that sponsors ensure that each investigator's verbatim terms are coded to standardized, preferred terms specified in a coding convention or dictionary. Proper coding allows similar events that were reported using different verbatim language to be appropriately grouped.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors should use a single coding convention or dictionary version throughout the clinical program and should not analyze adverse event data using one version and then base proposed labeling on a different version.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A - Describing Adverse Events to Identify Safety Signals
rules.verbatim_quote: It is not advisable to analyze adverse event data using one version and then base proposed labeling on a different version.
rules.exceptions: If revision of the coding convention is unavoidable, sponsors must appropriately group and analyze adverse events taking into account the revisions in subsequent versions.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must confirm that verbatim terms used by investigators have been appropriately coded, including identifying instances where a potentially serious verbatim term may have been inappropriately mapped to a more benign coding term.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A.1 - Accuracy of Coding
rules.verbatim_quote: We recommend that in addition to ensuring that investigators have accurately characterized adverse events, sponsors confirm that verbatim terms used by investigators have been appropriately coded. Sponsors should strive to identify obvious coding mistakes as well as any instances when a potentially serious verbatim term may have been inappropriately mapped to a more benign coding term.

rules.rule_text: Prior to analyzing a product's safety database, sponsors must ensure that adverse events were coded with minimal variability across studies and individual coders.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A.1 - Accuracy of Coding
rules.verbatim_quote: Prior to analyzing a product's safety database, sponsors should ensure that adverse events were coded with minimal variability across studies and individual coders.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors should prospectively group adverse event terms and develop case definitions or use accepted standardized definitions whenever possible, in consultation with the Agency.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A.2 - Coding Considerations During Adverse Event Analysis
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors, in consultation with the Agency, should prospectively group adverse event terms and develop case definitions or use accepted standardized definitions whenever possible.
rules.exceptions: Some groupings can be constructed only after safety data are obtained, at which time consultation with FDA might be considered.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must explain adverse event groupings explicitly in their applications so that FDA reviewers have a clear understanding of what terms were grouped and the rationale for the groupings.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A.2 - Coding Considerations During Adverse Event Analysis
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should explain such groupings explicitly in their applications so that FDA reviewers have a clear understanding of what terms were grouped and the rationale for the groupings.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors should analyze event rates by dose for clinically important adverse events that may be product-related and events that might be expected based on a product's pharmacologic class or preclinical data.
rules.source_section: Section VI.C - Analyzing Dose Effect as a Contribution to Risk Assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should analyze event rates by dose for clinically important adverse events that may be product related and events that might be expected based on a product's pharmacologic class or preclinical data.

rules.rule_text: When pooling safety data across studies, sponsors must confirm that study designs, as well as ascertainment and measurement strategies employed in the studies that are pooled, are reasonably similar.
rules.source_section: Section VI.D - Role of Data Pooling in Risk Assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: To ensure that pooling is appropriate, sponsors should confirm that study designs, as well as ascertainment and measurement strategies employed in the studies that are pooled, are reasonably similar.

rules.rule_text: All placebo-controlled studies in a clinical development program should be considered and evaluated for appropriateness for inclusion in a pooled safety analysis.
rules.source_section: Section VI.E - Using Pooled Data for Risk Assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: All placebo-controlled studies in a clinical development program should be considered and evaluated for appropriateness for inclusion in a pooled analysis.
rules.exceptions: Phase 1 pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic studies should generally be excluded from pooled analyses.

rules.rule_text: The risk of a safety outcome of interest in pooled analyses should be expressed in reference to total person-time (exposure time) or be evaluated using a time-to-event analysis when drug exposure duration varies across subjects.
rules.source_section: Section VI.E - Using Pooled Data for Risk Assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: The risk of the safety outcome of interest should be expressed in reference to total person-time (exposure time) or be evaluated using a time-to-event analysis.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must attempt to account for all dropouts in clinical trials, including rigorously ascertaining the reasons for withdrawal, particularly whether a safety issue contributed to discontinuation.
rules.source_section: Section VI.F - Rigorous Ascertainment of Reasons for Withdrawals from Studies
rules.verbatim_quote: Regardless of the reason for withdrawal, sponsors should attempt to account for all dropouts. Sponsors should try to ascertain what precipitated dropout or withdrawal in all cases, particularly if a safety issue was a part of the reason for withdrawal.

rules.rule_text: Participants who leave a study because of serious or significant safety issues must be followed closely until the adverse events are fully and permanently resolved or stabilized, with follow-up data recorded in the case report forms.
rules.source_section: Section VI.F - Rigorous Ascertainment of Reasons for Withdrawals from Studies
rules.verbatim_quote: Participants who leave a study because of serious or significant safety issues should be followed closely until the adverse events are fully and permanently resolved or stabilized (if complete resolution is not anticipated), with follow-up data recorded in the case report forms.

rules.rule_text: For drugs with very long half-lives, drugs deposited in organs, or drugs with potential for irreversible effects such as cancer, all subjects should be followed to the end of the study or even after the formal end of the study for long-term safety events.
rules.source_section: Section VI.G - Long-term Follow-up
rules.verbatim_quote: In some cases, it is recommended that all subjects be followed to the end of the study or even after the formal end of the study (e.g., where the drug has a very long half-life, is deposited in an organ such as bone or brain, or has the potential for causing irreversible effects, such as cancer).
rules.exceptions: The duration of follow-up is dependent on the circumstances of the product development.

rules.rule_text: For drugs that are new members of an established class, the adverse events that are important for the class of drug must be fully characterized in the NDA's integrated summary of safety.
rules.source_section: Section VI.H - Important Aspects of Data Presentation
rules.verbatim_quote: For a drug that is a new member of an established class of drugs, the adverse events that are important for the class of drug should be fully characterized in the NDA's integrated summary of safety.

rules.rule_text: Case report forms submitted for patients who died or discontinued a study prematurely due to an adverse event must include copies of relevant hospital records, autopsy reports, biopsy reports, and radiological reports.
rules.source_section: Section VI.H - Important Aspects of Data Presentation
rules.verbatim_quote: Case report forms (CRFs) submitted for patients who died or discontinued a study prematurely due to an adverse event should include copies of relevant hospital records, autopsy reports, biopsy reports, and radiological reports, when feasible.
rules.exceptions: Such information should be included when feasible.

rules.rule_text: Narrative summaries of important adverse events (e.g., deaths, events leading to discontinuation, other serious adverse events) must provide the detail necessary to permit an adequate understanding of the nature of the adverse event experienced by the study subject.
rules.source_section: Section VI.H - Important Aspects of Data Presentation
rules.verbatim_quote: Narrative summaries (as previously described in guidance) of important adverse events (e.g., deaths, events leading to discontinuation, other serious adverse events) should provide the detail necessary to permit an adequate understanding of the nature of the adverse event experienced by the study subject.
rules.exceptions: This level of detail may be unnecessary for events expected in the population (e.g., late deaths in a cancer trial).

rules.rule_text: Premarketing assessments must identify all medication errors that occur during product development and assess the resultant risk in the context of how and in whom the product will be used.
rules.source_section: Section V.B - Medication Errors
rules.verbatim_quote: Premarketing assessments should focus on: Identifying all medication errors that occur during product development; Identifying the reasons or causes for each identified error (e.g., dosage form, packaging, labeling, or confusion due to trade names when written or spoken); Assessing the resultant risk in the context of how and in whom the product will be used; Identifying the means to minimize, reduce or eliminate the medication errors by ensuring the proper naming, labeling, design, and packaging of the product.

rules.rule_text: Medication errors and other safety events that occurred or were considered during product development must be documented, reported, and analyzed for potential remedial actions.
rules.source_section: Section V.B - Medication Errors
rules.verbatim_quote: or considered during product development should be documented, reported, and analyzed for potential remedial actions (e.g., redesign of the device or modification of instructions for use).

rules.rule_text: Temporal analyses of adverse events must be conducted for important adverse events when there is prior experience suggesting a temporal relationship exists between product exposure and the adverse event.
rules.source_section: Section VI.B - Analyzing Temporal or Other Associations
rules.verbatim_quote: Analyses of temporal associations are particularly worth conducting in situations where prior experience (e.g., experience from similar products) has shown that a temporal relationship between product exposure and ensuing adverse events is likely to exist.

rules.rule_text: The distribution of important variables across pooled data — including sex, age, extent of exposure, concomitant medical conditions, and concomitant medications — must be included in the integrated summary of safety.
rules.source_section: Section VI.H - Important Aspects of Data Presentation
rules.verbatim_quote: The distribution of important variables across the pooled data, such as sex, age, extent of exposure, concomitant medical conditions, and concomitant medications (especially those that are used commonly to treat the indication being studied), should be included in the integrated summary of safety.

rules.rule_text: When there is clinical heterogeneity among trials with regard to the safety outcome of interest, sponsors must present risk information that details the range of results observed in the individual studies rather than only a summary pooled value.
rules.source_section: Section VI.D - Role of Data Pooling in Risk Assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: When there is clinical heterogeneity among trials with regard to the safety outcome of interest (e.g., major disparity in findings for particular safety endpoints), sponsors should present risk information that details the range of results observed in the individual studies, rather than producing a summary value from a pooled analysis.

rules.rule_text: For gene-based biological products, sponsors must assess transfection of nontarget cells, transmissibility of infection to close contacts, and the genetic stability of products intended for long-persistence transfections as important safety issues.
rules.source_section: Section V.C - Addressing Safety Aspects During Product Development
rules.verbatim_quote: For gene-based biological products, transfection of nontarget cells and transmissibility of infection to close contacts, and the genetic stability of products intended for long-persistence transfections constitute important safety issues.

rules.rule_text: For cell-based products, sponsors must assess adverse events related to distribution, migration, and growth beyond the initial intended administration site, as well as adverse events related to cell survival and demise.
rules.source_section: Section V.C - Addressing Safety Aspects During Product Development
rules.verbatim_quote: For cell-based products, assessments of adverse events related to distribution, migration, and growth beyond the initial intended administration are important, as are adverse events related to cell survival and demise. Such events may not appear for a long time after product administration.

rules.rule_text: If an adverse event is mischaracterized, sponsors must consult with FDA before recharacterizing the event; recharacterization must be the exception rather than the rule and must be well documented with an audit trail.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A.1 - Accuracy of Coding
rules.verbatim_quote: If an adverse event is mischaracterized, sponsors could consider, in consultation with FDA, recharacterizing the event to make it consistent with accepted case definitions. We recommend that recharacterization be the exception rather than the rule and, when done, be well documented with an audit trail.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors should analyze dose-response relationships by subgroup when specific demographic or baseline disease-related subgroups may be at particular risk of incurring adverse events.
rules.source_section: Section VI.C - Analyzing Dose Effect as a Contribution to Risk Assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: In addition, when specific demographic or baseline disease-related subgroups may be at particular risk of incurring adverse events, exploration of dose-response relationships by subgroup is important.
rules.exceptions: Multiplicity issues could result in an apparent signal that does not represent a real finding (false positive).

rules.rule_text: Sponsors should use a Failure Mode and Effects Analysis or equivalent structured technique to assess and prevent medication errors during product development.
rules.source_section: Section V.B - Medication Errors
rules.verbatim_quote: Depending on the nature of the product, the indication, how it is administered, who will be receiving it, and the context in which it will be used, one or more of the following techniques may be helpful in assessing and preventing medication errors: Conducting a Failure Mode and Effects Analysis.
rules.exceptions: Other techniques including use of expert panels, computer-assisted analysis, direct observation during clinical trials, directed interviews, focus groups, and simulated prescription and OTC use studies may be used as alternatives or complements.

rules.rule_text: The effect of differential discontinuation rates by treatment on adverse event occurrence must be characterized so as to avoid misleading adverse event incidences when patient exposure is not used as the denominator for risk calculations.
rules.source_section: Section VI.H - Important Aspects of Data Presentation
rules.verbatim_quote: The effect of differential discontinuation rates by treatment on adverse event occurrence should be characterized (e.g., when placebo-treated patients drop out of a trial earlier than patients being treated with an active drug). This differential discontinuation can lead to misleading adverse event incidences unless patient exposure is used as the denominator for risk calculations.
